(almost) everything is out now, and the stage is set for Whalefall. Looking forward to it.

items.title:
We URGENTLY need a federal law forbidding AI from impersonating humans

The night before I testified...
items.content:
We URGENTLY need a federal law forbidding AI from impersonating humans

The night before I testified in the US Senate in May, 2023, the late philosopher Daniel Dennett sent me a manuscript that he called “counterfeit people”. It was published a few days later in The Atlantic. 

It started like this

“MONEY HAS EXISTED for several thousand years, and from the outset counterfeiting was recognized to be a very serious crime, one that in many cases calls for capital punishment because it undermines the trust on which society depends. Today, for the first time in history, thanks to artificial intelligence, it is possible for anybody to make counterfeit people who can pass for real in many of the new digital environments we have created. These counterfeit people are the most dangerous artifacts in human history, capable of destroying not just economies but human freedom itself. Before it's too late (it may well be too late already) we must outlaw both the creation of counterfeit people and the "passing along" of counterfeit people. The penalties for either offense should be extremely severe, given that civilization itself is at risk.”

He was right then. Three years later even more so. The need for the kind of law he was calling for – one forbidding “the creation of and ‘passing along’ of counterfeit people” is now urgent.

Two items sent to me this morning make that absolutely clear, a deep fake video you have probably seen of Tom Cruise fighting Brad Pitt, and a tool to hook up OpenClaw to voice synthesis.

Scammers will be among the first to adopt these tools. And indeed they already have; a friend who was filming me for a documentary yesterday told me of a Canadian friend of his who was scammed out of hundreds of thousands of dollars by a deepfaked video of Mark Carney. Because the tools for counterfeiting have gotten so good 2026 will almost certainly see more deepfaked scams like this than the rest of history combined.

Tell your representatives today, not tomorrow, that we must pass federal laws forbidding machine output from being presented as humans, and that we must develop the means to enforce those laws. No use of the first person by chatbots, and no more deepfakes of living people’s voices and images without their express consent, aside from carveouts for obvious parody and so on. All of this has gone too far, too fast. And we must not let corporate lobbyists thwart efforts to address all this.

Generative AI systems may still struggle to reason, but they were built for mimicry, and their mimicry has gotten to the point where we must do something now. 

I will end by quoting from Dennett’s deeply prescient Atlantic essay (and I am sorry that it is paywalled):

Frontier model performance remains largely tied to a familiar input distribution.

Performance remains tied to familiar input distributions.
items.reasoning: Important finding from the creator of ARC benchmark himself. Demonstrates that high benchmark scores may not reflect genuine reasoning - a critical issue for the field's evaluation methodology.

items.title: Here are my slides from my recent CMU talk, as I'm transitioning from the Olmo 3 era of just buildin...
items.content:
Here are my slides from my recent CMU talk, as I'm transitioning from the Olmo 3 era of just building a reasoning model to thinking about how to do impactful research for agentic systems. 

In particular, being on a campus again was great to experience the intellectual vibrancy and curiosity that has a bit of a different flavor from AI labs -- when you're totally free of the stress from needing to think about how your research improves the next generation(s) of models, at least somewhat indirectly. There's a different stress to being a student today (opportunity costs), but it gives me confidence in the value of training and fundamentals you can still get.

The deck has some tightened content from olmo 3, throughput comparisons in RL for different architectures, and a bunch of things that we're up to on post-training to get ready the next generation of Olmos. 

I still have a bunch more to figure out before I make my next big bet on where to spend my time for Olmo 4. 

https://t.co/VkPZpXcpv6

items.title:
Some people might say, "but aren't humans also very sensitive to encoding format?"

In my opinion, f...
items.content:
Some people might say, "but aren't humans also very sensitive to encoding format?"

In my opinion, for an actually intelligent agent, re-encoding a task with a known encoding scheme should always be a no-op for performance.

If you give me a set of multiplications to do, but you've encoded the values in binary, my first action will be to decode them back to the format I'm familiar with - digits. Because "decode binary" and "multiply" are simple and error-correctable operations, final performance should be 100% all of the time.

In fact you could chain many such indirection steps and still see absolutely 0 loss.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues that truly intelligent agents should be invariant to input encoding format changes, since decoding is a simple composable operation that shouldn't degrade performance.
items.reasoning: Technical insight from Keras creator about the nature of reasoning vs. pattern matching. Philosophically important for understanding model limitations.
items.summary_html: Chollet argues that truly intelligent agents should be invariant to input encoding format changes, since decoding is a simple composable operation that shouldn't degrade performance.
items.content_html:

If you give me a set of multiplications to do, but you've encoded the values in binary, my first action will be to decode them back to the format I'm familiar with - digits. Because "decode binary" and "multiply" are simple and error-correctable operations, final performance should be 100% all of the time.

In fact you could chain many such indirection steps and still see absolutely 0 loss.

items.title:
So far, AI can only reliably do the job of an average CEO:

1. You participate in transcribed Teams ...
items.content:
So far, AI can only reliably do the job of an average CEO:

1. You participate in transcribed Teams meetings, let everyone speak, then ask the AI to generate actionable items and send them to the participants.

2. You ask the AI to read news for your industry vertical, compare it to your strategic plan, and tell you what you should change in the plan.

3. You feed the AI your company's quarterly metrics and last board deck, and it generates an updated board presentation with the three "strategic priorities" that sound new but are actually the same ones from last quarter, reworded.

4. From time to time ask the AI to select a couple of folks to fire based on the yearly reviews.

items.title:
Speculative decoding is not just "throughput optimization".

In production systems, the real impact ...
items.content:
Speculative decoding is not just "throughput optimization".

In production systems, the real impact of speculative decoding is on tail latency.

Speculative decoding pairs your main model with a smaller, faster draft model.

The draft model proposes tokens; the main model verifies them in parallel. When the draft model predicts well, you can skip serial generation steps and reduce latency.

But there's a problem:

A generic draft model wasn't trained on your data, so they make many mistakes, and the main model will reject most of their predictions.

At this point, any potential latency benefits are gone.

The fix is to post-train a custom draft model on your production data.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino explains speculative decoding in production: it pairs a main model with a smaller draft model for parallel token verification. The key insight is that generic draft models perform poorly on specific data, so post-training custom draft models on production data is essential.
items.reasoning: Good technical educational content on speculative decoding with practical production insights. Decent engagement. Actionable for ML engineers.

items.title: @abakermi @hthieblot @fdotinc I'm going through each of the hundred over the next week or so. I don'...
items.content:
@abakermi @hthieblot @fdotinc I'm going through each of the hundred over the next week or so. I don't look at the world that way; I look at the world and ask: does it have a need to exist anymore? 

Most of these do. They each offer something, though whether they survive or not has a lot of intangibles to it. It's just too hard to figure out which ones of these are going to survive five years. Most companies that don't exist simply don't find customers. So, if they find customers, they exist. If they don't, they go away.

When voice AI feels robotic, it’s usually not the voice.

It’s the delay.

In real conversation, we don’t wait politely for 1 second pauses.
We interrupt, react, change direction mid-sentence.

So the real bar for voice AI is simple:

→ respond fast enough to feel instinctive

items.title:
It might be the caffeine speaking, but I love you all ❤️

Happy Valentine's day you sexy mf'ers.

I'...
items.content:
It might be the caffeine speaking, but I love you all ❤️

Happy Valentine's day you sexy mf'ers.

I'm writing this at a coffeeshop. Rain outside. A first date happening next to me, I'm hoping they make it.

I'm feeling happy & grateful for all of this, human civilization, life on Earth, a spinning rock in space. There are 100-400 billion other planets in our galaxy alone. And there are ~2 trillion galaxies in just the observable universe.

And somehow we have a chance to crack open the mysteries of the universe, to solve physics, biology, intelligence, to understand our own mind, to travel out toward the stars.

And at the same time, we often bicker about the stupidest shit. The whole thing is hilarious and beautiful.

All of this is a miracle ❤️

PS: It's definitely the caffeine speaking 🤣

items.title:
energy this week hasn't been bad, about 6-7 out of 10 for the most part

biggest difference was wear...
items.content:
energy this week hasn't been bad, about 6-7 out of 10 for the most part

biggest difference was wearing a mask everywhere and switch out furniture causing inflammation

there were one or two meals where I felt tired afterwards, indicating there were likely allergens: these are at nice restaurants where I specifically requested no gluten, dairy, eggs, seed oils, so likely a cross contamination issue (unfortunately you should assume this unless strong evidence points otherwise)

so far the safest restaurants i found: any good hotpot restaurant where you request only hot water for the base, don't use any sauce, just eat their fresh meat and veggies -- you can go pretty far making your own soup base by throwing veggies and salt into the plain water (I hear about haidilao guides on rednote but mostly just winged it myself)
items.author: @TheGregYang
items.summary: Greg Yang shares detailed health optimization notes about allergens, inflammation, and restaurant dining strategies.
items.reasoning: Entirely off-topic personal health content.
items.summary_html: Greg Yang shares detailed health optimization notes about allergens, inflammation, and restaurant dining strategies.
items.content_html:
energy this week hasn't been bad, about 6-7 out of 10 for the most part

biggest difference was wearing a mask everywhere and switch out furniture causing inflammation

there were one or two meals where I felt tired afterwards, indicating there were likely allergens: these are at nice restaurants where I specifically requested no gluten, dairy, eggs, seed oils, so likely a cross contamination issue (unfortunately you should assume this unless strong evidence points otherwise)

so far the safest restaurants i found: any good hotpot restaurant where you request only hot water for the base, don't use any sauce, just eat their fresh meat and veggies -- you can go pretty far making your own soup base by throwing veggies and salt into the plain water (I hear about haidilao guides on rednote but mostly just winged it myself)
